pattern: Divisors of a number appear in ascending order the moment you test candidates from one upward, so a straightforward counting pass finds the k-th one without needing to sort anything.
hints: You don't need to collect every factor up front -- you can count them as you find them.
Walk candidate divisors from 1 up to n itself, and every time one divides n evenly, that's the next factor in ascending order.
Stop as soon as your running count reaches k and return that candidate; if you finish the walk without reaching k, there weren't enough factors, so return -1.

edge_inputs.description: statement example, prime n's second factor is itself
statement example, k exceeds factor count
large prime n, second factor is n itself
k lands exactly one past the last real factor
perfect square n, k lands on the square root factor
n=1000 has far fewer factors than k requests

stress.description: random n with k always within that n's valid factor-index range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    k = rng.randint(1, n)
    return [n, k]
